# case98 — library build-setting difference, not a consumer language-floor raise (NO_CHANGE)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Verdict** | ✅ **NO_CHANGE** |
| **Category** | No Change |
| **Platforms** | Linux, macOS, Windows |
| **Flags** | — |
| **Detected `ChangeKind`s** | — |
| **Source files** | `examples/case98_cxx_standard_floor_raised/` |

## What this case demonstrates

`v1.h` and `v2.h` declare an identical public surface and neither uses any
post-C++17 construct — a consumer TU including `v2.h` compiles unchanged
under `-std=c++17` with both GCC and Clang (verified directly; see
`v2.h`'s Reproduce note). **The case's directory name is aspirational, not
descriptive**: nothing here actually raises the C++ standard a *consumer*
must build with. The only real difference is the *library's own* build
contract: `v2`'s `.so` is compiled with `-std=c++20` (via
`V2_COMPILE_OPTIONS` in `CMakeLists.txt`) while the public declaration set
stays byte-identical to `v1`.

## Why per-binary detection cannot see this

The symbol set, vtable, and declared interface are unchanged between
v1 and v2, so every conventional ABI diff returns `NO_CHANGE`. The
break-grade signal only appears when abicheck is fed a *matrix* of
per-configuration snapshots (one per consumer toolchain): the
`CXX_STANDARD_FLOOR_RAISED` detector compares the minimum `cxx_std`
across configurations and emits the finding when the floor moves up.

The probe-harness mechanism that produces matrix snapshots lives in
`abicheck.probe_harness`; it is not yet wired into the default
`compare` CLI (deferred follow-up — see PR #247).

## Expected verdict

`NO_CHANGE` — single-config diff is correctly silent, and it is the *right*
answer here: there is no consumer-visible declaration or language-floor
change to report, only a library-internal build-flag difference. The case
is kept as a fixture for a future detector channel that would record and
compare each side's build `cxx_std` directly (matrix/probe-harness), which
is a different, narrower signal than "the library needs a newer language
standard to consume."

---

- `CMakeLists.txt`
- `app.cpp`

- `v1.h`
- `v2.cpp`
- `v2.h`
